text.fullText: Docusign Envelope ID: 5CF24D5E-4976-4901-85B1-8ECC70E430BA February 17, 2026 The Honorable Lee Zeldin Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Ave., NW Washington, DC 20460 Submitted via regulations.gov: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Subject: U.S. EPA’s Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Docket Number: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Administrator Zeldin: The Division of Water Resources (Division) of the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality (NCDEQ) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposal to revise the existing Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification Rule (Proposed Rule). The mission of the NCDEQ is to provide science-based environmental stewardship for the health and prosperity of all North Carolinians. Within NCDEQ, the Division is responsible for issuing 401 Water Quality Certifications within the state of North Carolina. North Carolina’s certification program conducts a significant number of reviews for proposed impacts and discharges to waters and wetlands of the State. In fiscal year 2024-2025, the Division reviewed 2,534 requests for 401 Certifications. This large permit volume handled by the Division underscores the Division’s familiarity with this issue and highlights the State’s established interest in protecting waters and wetlands in North Carolina. In the Division’s view, the current Section 401 process is working well and does not need to be overhauled again, which would avoid additional regulatory uncertainty for applicants and implementation disruptions for state agencies. Drawing on its experience implementing North Carolina’s water quality programs, the Division offers the following comments on the Proposed Rule: Request for Certification The Proposed Rule modifies the items needed in a request for certification at 40 CFR 121.5. Specifically, the Agency proposes the removal of 40 CFR 121.5(c), which currently allows a state to require additional information before the request is considered complete. The Proposed Rule limits the list of items for a complete application to essentially two items for the request to be deemed complete and the Reasonable Period of Time clock to start: 1. A copy of the Federal license or permit application submitted to the Federal agency; and Docusign Envelope ID: 5CF24D5E-4976-4901-85B1-8ECC70E430BA U.S. EPA’s Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Docket Number: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comments by NC DEQ - Division of Water Resources Page 2 of 4 2. Any readily available water quality-related materials that informed the development of the application. This truncated list is much shorter than the items required for a complete certification request when the EPA Regional Administrator is the certifying authority and removes current rule efficiencies in the application process. This change will necessarily result in longer review times as this missing information must be requested separately by the certifying authority. 40 CFR 121.5(b) contains seven items that constitute a complete certification request when EPA is certifying, whereas the Proposed Rule has only two items for certifying authorities (i.e. States and Tribes with Treatment as a State). North Carolina’s rules include eleven items that constitute a complete certification request (15A NCAC 02H). All these items are necessary for North Carolina to review the request and determine compliance with applicable state water quality requirements. They include many common-sense items, such as a map to delineate the boundaries of the land utilized by the applicant in carrying out the activity and the location and extent of the receiving waters within the boundaries of the lands. (Coincidentally, the Proposed Rule also considers this data necessary when EPA acts as the certifying authority.) In North Carolina, the result will be that applicant will have to supplement their Federally “complete” request for certification with additional information to satisfy state rule. This will require staff to review the application materials and request the missing information resulting in delays and inefficiencies. The Federal “Reasonable Period of Time” clock will have already started resulting in less time for the applicant to provide the information and less time for the State to review the submittal and provide a certification decision. If the State does not receive this information in a timely manner, the result of this rule change may unintentionally lead to increased certification denials due to insufficient information. Consistent with the principal of States’ primacy in the Clean Water Act (i.e. State’s know their water resources and what is needed to comply with state-specific water quality requirements) and to simplify the certification process for applicants. North Carolina strongly urges EPA not to remove the current rule provisions at 40 CFR 121.5(c). Scope of Certification The Proposed Rule requests comments on the definition of water quality requirements and asks if this definition should be limited to only numeric water quality criteria or items in Section 401(d) of the Clean Water Act. The Division objects to defining water quality requirements to only numeric water quality criteria. Narrative water quality criteria are necessary to achieve protection of designated uses and are developed in situations in which the numeric criteria alone cannot ensure that designed uses can be achieved. In the Proposed Rule preamble, EPA rightly recognizes that the definition of water requirements extends to water quality-related provisions of State or Tribal law. These requirements should not be limited to only EPA-approved provisions to allow States to protect their water quality in the most Docusign Envelope ID: 5CF24D5E-4976-4901-85B1-8ECC70E430BA U.S. EPA’s Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Docket Number: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comments by NC DEQ - Division of Water Resources Page 3 of 4 efficient manner, rather than relying on multiple regulatory programs to achieve the same result. The rule also proposes to change the scope of 401 Certification review from the “activity” to review of the “point source discharge” associated with the activity. Learning from our extensive experience implementing this program water quality can be impacted in ways that are not directly attributable to a point source discharge. Aquatic systems respond not only to discrete discharges, but to cumulative, indirect, and interacting stressors, such as hydrologic alteration, habitat fragmentation, and incremental wetland losses. If the whole project’s impacts on waters are not reviewed under the 401 Certification program, water quality standard violations may occur even if the project receives certification. The Proposed Rule maintains the requirement for Federal agency programmatic actions (like U.S. Army Corps of Engineers’ nationwide permits), as well as agency-led projects (like U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Civil Works projects), to receive 401 Certifications. However, EPA requests comment on whether this is the “best read” of the statute. The Division believes there is no statutory basis for excluding this action from 401 reviews. Further, we strongly request that EPA retain this requirement for Federal actions to obtain 401 Certification to protect North Carolina’s aquatic resources. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Civil Works division regularly conducts large-scale dredging operations in North Carolina’s coastal waters. These waters are home to primary nursery areas and habitat for many commercial and recreational fish species, unique and diverse coastal wetlands that house endangered species, the drinking water source for millions of North Carolinians, and a foundation of North Carolina’s economic prosperity. State review of these projects under the 401 program is critical to ensure Federal activities will not impair these protected uses. Lastly, the Division requests that EPA clearly and affirmatively state—either in the regulatory text or, at a minimum, in the preamble—that replacing “project proponent” with “applicant” is not intended to limit the applicability of Section 401of the CWA. Modifications The Proposed Rule intends to allow certifying authorities to modify grants of certification only in circumstances in which the applicant agrees on the exact language of the modification. This is unreasonable and unnecessarily bureaucratic. In North Carolina, nearly all substantive modifications to 401 Certifications are initiated by the applicant, often because of changes in the project during construction. This approach hinders the State’s flexibility in working with applicants to protect water quality due to project changes without project delays or resorting to enforcement. In addition, the result of this Proposed Rule change may be protracted back and forth between applicants and certifying authorities on precise language, decreasing efficient operation of the 401 Certification program. North Carolina often communicates with applicants during the development of the 401 Certification and its conditions so that there are no surprises or objections to the language in the Certification. However, applicants have Docusign Envelope ID: 5CF24D5E-4976-4901-85B1-8ECC70E430BA U.S. EPA’s Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Docket Number: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comments by NC DEQ - Division of Water Resources Page 4 of 4 recourse available if they do object to the contents of a Certification already under North Carolina law. Lastly, the Proposed Rule’s position that 401 Certification decisions cannot be modified is unreasonable and conflicts with the CWA itself and corresponding North Carolina state rules which allows limited situations in which a 401 Certification may be revoked or modified. North Carolina’s authority to modify a 401 Certification decision is limited to very few, but necessary, circumstances. If information contained in the application on which the State based their decision is erroneous, or if the project violates other provisions of the Clean Water Act or State water law, North Carolina law allows changes to the 401 Certification decision. These circumstances have arisen rarely in North Carolina’s 401 program history but are a necessary authority for the operation of the state’s water quality protection. Finalizing a Federal rule that contradicts this authority would likely result in North Carolina having to pursue rulemaking to align discrepancy and may limit the State’s ability to curtail projects that violate water quality rules. Conclusion In conclusion, the Division does not believe there are any changes needed to the current regulations implementing Section 401 in effect today. If the Agency moves forward with the Proposed Rule, we request that the publication of a final rule includes an effective date of at least 270 days or more after publication to give the State ample time to understand the changes, adopt State rules and regulatory programs to these changes, and to develop guidance for regulated customers in North Carolina. The Federal authorities have changed the 401 Certification Rule in 2020, 2023 and now proposed to again in 2026, resulting in regulatory confusion, court challenges, and inconsistencies for certifying authorities and the regulated community across the nation. Staff in North Carolina’s 401 Certification program are already receiving questions about the Proposed Rule and what the changes mean for projects within the state. It is imperative to allow certifying authorities and developers plenty of time to adapt. We carry the responsibility of communicating the impacts of these latest rule changes to our customers in North Carolina and must have appropriate time to do so. We know from past Federal rule changes, that robust and thoughtful outreach is necessary to ensure compliance and efficient operation of the 401 Water Quality Certification program. The Division appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on EPA’s proposal to revise the existing CWA Section 401 Water Quality Certification rule. We thank you for your consideration of these comments. Sincerely, Richard E. Rogers Jr., Director Division of Water Resources North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality
